planting.note: Plant corms just below the soil surface in humus-rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in shade; unlike other hardy cyclamen that require dry summer dormancy, C. purpurascens remains in leaf year-round and should receive moisture through summer as well as winter. It establishes slowly from corms but is very long-lived once settled.

directions: Plant corms in shade in humus-rich, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil; European cyclamen is uniquely distinct from the other two commonly grown hardy cyclamen species (C. hederifolium and C. coum) in two important ways — it is the only hardy cyclamen that blooms in summer (July and August) rather than autumn or winter, and it is the only one that does not require a dry summer dormancy period, remaining in leaf and active year-round. The flowers are sweetly fragrant (unlike most hardy cyclamen), in shades of pale to deep rose-pink, with the typical reflexed, swept-back petals of the genus. The rounded, dark-green leaves are beautifully marbled with silver patterns and are never lost — the plant is essentially evergreen. Native to the shaded limestone woodlands of the Alps and Apennines in central Europe, it requires cool, moist, slightly alkaline conditions under deciduous trees. Slower to establish and rarer in cultivation than C. hederifolium, but uniquely valuable for its summer bloom, fragrance, and year-round leaf retention.

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring or plant container-grown plants in moist to dry, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; this hybrid is one of the most drought-tolerant epimediums once established. Cut old foliage back to the ground in late winter (February) before the new flowers and leaves emerge to display the early spring show.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part to full shade; Perralchicum barrenwort is one of the most vigorous, robust, and drought-tolerant epimediums — a hybrid between E. perralderianum (Algerian barrenwort) and E. pinnatum subsp. colchicum, producing broadly heart-shaped, dark-green, leathery leaves that are semi-evergreen to evergreen and form a dense, reliable groundcover under trees where few other plants will grow. In early spring, before the foliage, slender wiry stems carry loose clusters of small, bright-yellow, four-petaled flowers with short spurs — like a miniature rain of golden stars above the crown. The primary cultivar 'Frohnleiten' is widely available and particularly vigorous. It spreads steadily by rhizomes and is among the most effective ground covers for dry shade under large trees — tolerating the challenging conditions of root competition, deep shade, and summer drought that defeat even other epimediums. Once established, it requires essentially no maintenance beyond cutting back old foliage annually.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall where plants are to grow permanently — fresh seed germinates readily in cool conditions; Siberian corydalis has a deep taproot and resents transplanting once established. It self-sows freely around the parent plant when happy. After the spring flowering and seed set, the plant goes summer dormant and disappears completely until the following spring.

directions: Sow seeds in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade; Siberian corydalis is the largest and most architectural of the commonly grown corydalis species — a bold-leaved spring ephemeral native to Siberia and central Asia producing finely divided, glaucous, blue-gray-green foliage of remarkable quality and elegance, and in early to mid-spring, dense, upright clusters of substantial yellow flowers with dark-brown to purple inner petal markings at the tip of each floret. The flowers are significantly larger than those of C. lutea or C. ochroleuca, the clusters more imposing, and the whole effect more dramatically architectural. After setting seed, the plant disappears completely into summer dormancy — the site should be marked to avoid inadvertent disturbance. It combines beautifully with late-emerging perennials (hostas, ferns, daylilies) that will fill in as it goes dormant. Often uncommon in nurseries despite being easy from fresh seed. Self-sows modestly to maintain a colony once established.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun, or propagate by division or cuttings. Broad-leaf thyme is more cold-hardy and more tolerant of clay soils than common thyme (T. vulgaris). Shear lightly after flowering to keep plants compact and promote fresh growth.

directions: Plant in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; broad-leaf thyme is a more robust and cold-hardy thyme species than the common culinary thyme (Thymus vulgaris), producing larger, broader, oval leaves with a strong, sweet thyme fragrance and flavor used interchangeably with common thyme in cooking. Native to limestone hills and rocky grasslands of central and western Europe, it forms a semi-shrubby, spreading mound of woody stems with soft, gray-green to dark-green, aromatic leaves and clusters of pink-purple flowers in summer that are magnets for bees — particularly bumblebees and honeybees. The culinary quality of the leaves is excellent and often considered superior to common thyme in flavor intensity. It is more tolerant of heavier soils and colder winters than Thymus vulgaris, making it a better choice for gardens in zones 4–5 where common thyme may winterkill. Several cultivars exist including 'Foxley' (silver-variegated), 'Kurt' (very compact), and 'Oregano-Scented' (with marjoram-like notes).

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; gentian sage develops tuberous roots that can be lifted and stored frost-free like dahlias in zones colder than zone 8, or left in the ground where it is perennial. Tubers are often readily available and give a head start over seed. The cultivar 'Cambridge Blue' is a pale sky-blue form; 'Oxford Blue' is the deep cobalt blue of the species.

directions: Start indoors early or plant tubers in full sun after danger of frost passes; gentian sage produces one of the most intensely cobalt-blue flowers in horticulture — large, open-mouthed, hooded flowers of deep, saturated gentian-blue (hence the common name) appearing in pairs along upright stems from summer through frost, each bloom substantially larger than most salvias and in a blue so vivid it registers as startling in the summer border. The species color is deep cobalt-blue; the cultivar 'Cambridge Blue' produces a softer, cleaner, sky-blue that some find more elegant. Both are remarkable for the purity and intensity of the blue. A Mexican native, it develops tuberous roots and is perennial in zones 8–11, where it can form substantial, long-lived clumps. In colder climates it is treated as a tender perennial or grown from tubers started indoors and lifted after frost like dahlias. Outstanding combined with orange Hemerocallis, yellow Rudbeckia, or white flowers that will contrast with the cobalt blue.
